quiz.title: Unit 4 Quiz 9, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Advanced Lewis Structures and Bonding Concepts
quiz.subject: Chemistry

questions.question: Which of these inert gases is the most reactive?
questions.explanation: Xenon is the most reactive among the noble gases. Although noble gases are generally unreactive due to their full valence shells, the heavier ones like xenon can form compounds under certain conditions because their outer electrons are farther from the nucleus and less tightly held. Xenon forms compounds such as XeF₂, XeF₄, and XeF₆ with fluorine. Helium, neon, and argon are essentially inert and do not form stable compounds.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the Lewis structure for silicon tetrachloride (SiCl₄)?
options: Silicon with 8 electrons, four single bonds to chlorine atoms, and no lone pairs
Silicon with 10 electrons, four single bonds to chlorine, and one lone pair
Silicon with 6 electrons, three single bonds to chlorine, and one lone pair
Silicon with 8 electrons, two double bonds and two single bonds to chlorine
questions.explanation: SiCl₄ has silicon (Group 14) with 4 valence electrons. It forms four single bonds with four chlorine atoms, using all its electrons for bonding and leaving no lone pairs on silicon. Silicon achieves an octet through the four shared pairs. Each chlorine atom has three lone pairs.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the Lewis structure for sulfur hexafluoride (SF₆)?
options: Sulfur with 12 electrons, six single bonds to fluorine atoms, and no lone pairs
Sulfur with 8 electrons, six single bonds to fluorine, and expanded octet impossible
Sulfur with 14 electrons, four double bonds and two single bonds to fluorine
Sulfur with 8 electrons, three double bonds and three single bonds to fluorine
questions.explanation: SF₆ has sulfur with an expanded octet. Sulfur forms six single bonds with six fluorine atoms, using its 6 valence electrons and accommodating 12 electrons total. This is possible because sulfur is in Period 3 and can use d-orbitals. Each fluorine has three lone pairs.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the Lewis structure for boron trifluoride (BF₃)?
options: Boron with 6 electrons, three single bonds to fluorine atoms, and no lone pairs on boron
Boron with 8 electrons, three single bonds to fluorine, and one lone pair
Boron with 10 electrons, three double bonds to fluorine, and no lone pairs
Boron with 8 electrons, two single bonds and one double bond to fluorine
questions.explanation: BF₃ has boron (Group 13) with 3 valence electrons. It forms three single bonds with three fluorine atoms, giving boron only 6 electrons (incomplete octet). Boron is electron-deficient and acts as a Lewis acid. Each fluorine has three lone pairs.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the Lewis structure for carbon disulfide (CS₂)?
options: Carbon with 8 electrons, two double bonds to sulfur atoms, and no lone pairs on carbon
Carbon with 6 electrons, two single bonds to sulfur, and two lone pairs
Carbon with 10 electrons, two triple bonds to sulfur atoms
Carbon with 8 electrons, one double bond and one single bond to sulfur
questions.explanation: CS₂ is analogous to CO₂. Carbon forms two double bonds with sulfur atoms, giving carbon an octet (8 electrons). Each sulfur has two lone pairs. Sulfur, being in Period 3, can also accommodate expanded octets, but in CS₂ it follows the octet rule.

questions.question: The correct Lewis structure for PBr₃ shows:
options: Phosphorus with 10 electrons and three single bonds to bromine atoms
Phosphorus with 8 electrons, three single bonds to bromine, and one lone pair
Phosphorus with 6 electrons and three double bonds to bromine
Phosphorus with 8 electrons, three double bonds to bromine, and no lone pairs
questions.explanation: Phosphorus (Group 15) has 5 valence electrons. It forms three single bonds with three bromine atoms (using 3 electrons), leaving one lone pair (2 electrons). This gives phosphorus a total of 8 electrons (octet). Each bromine atom forms one single bond and has three lone pairs, completing its octet.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct Lewis structure for CH₂O?
options: Carbon with 8 electrons, two single bonds to hydrogen and one double bond to oxygen, with two lone pairs on oxygen
Carbon with 8 electrons, three single bonds to hydrogen and oxygen, with two lone pairs on oxygen
Carbon with 6 electrons, two double bonds to hydrogen and oxygen
Carbon with 8 electrons, one triple bond to oxygen and two single bonds to hydrogen
questions.explanation: CH₂O (formaldehyde) has carbon as the central atom. Carbon forms two single bonds with hydrogen atoms and one double bond with oxygen. This gives carbon an octet. Oxygen has two lone pairs. Each hydrogen has a duet.

questions.question: A lone pair is defined as:
options: A pair of electrons on the central atom
questions.explanation: A lone pair is a pair of valence electrons that are not shared with another atom in a covalent bond. They are also called non-bonding pairs and are shown as dots in Lewis structures.

questions.question: Which atom will never be in the middle of a Lewis Dot structure?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen (H) will never be the central atom in a Lewis structure. Hydrogen can only form one bond (needs only 2 electrons) and cannot serve as a central atom that forms multiple bonds. It is always terminal.

questions.question: Which type of bond results in the highest melting point?
questions.explanation: Covalent network bonds (as in diamond, silicon dioxide, or silicon carbide) result in the highest melting points. These substances form giant three-dimensional lattices held together by strong covalent bonds that require enormous amounts of energy to break. Ionic compounds have high melting points but generally lower than covalent networks. Covalent molecular substances (like H₂O or CO₂) have low melting points because only weak intermolecular forces need to be overcome. Metallic bonds vary widely but typically have lower melting points than covalent networks (though some metals like tungsten have very high melting points).

questions.question: Which of the following statements is False about atoms?
options: They may or may not be capable of free existence
They have equal number of protons and electrons
questions.explanation: Atoms are not charged particles; they are electrically neutral because they have equal numbers of protons and electrons. Statement D is false. Statement A is true (atoms are neutral), statement B is true (some atoms exist freely, others bond), and statement C is true (in a neutral atom, protons = electrons).

questions.question: These have delocalized electrons:
questions.explanation: Metallic bonds have delocalized electrons that move freely through the lattice of positive metal ions. This electron delocalization gives metals their conductivity and malleability. Some covalent structures (like graphite) also have delocalized electrons, but metallic bonding is defined by this property.

questions.question: These have very large electronegativity differences:
questions.explanation: Ionic bonds form between atoms with very large electronegativity differences (typically >1.7), where one atom completely transfers electrons to another. This creates ions held together by electrostatic attraction.

questions.question: Compared to ionic compounds, molecular compounds generally have:
questions.explanation: Molecular compounds (covalent) generally have low boiling points compared to ionic compounds. This is because they are held together by weaker intermolecular forces rather than strong ionic bonds. Ionic compounds require high temperatures to overcome their lattice energy.

questions.question: Compared to ionic compounds, molecular compounds generally have:
questions.explanation: Molecular compounds generally have poor conductivity because they consist of neutral molecules without free-moving charged particles. Ionic compounds conduct electricity when molten or dissolved, but not in solid state. Molecular compounds typically do not conduct in any state.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered advanced Lewis structures and bonding concepts! 🧪🔗
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review expanded octets and electron-deficient molecules like BF₃.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on lone pair definitions and comparing compound types.
scoreMessages.0: Please review Lewis structure rules, central atom selection, and properties of different bond types.
